name: Oenghus Extended
description: Oenghus, also known as Óengus mac ind Óg (Óengus son of the Young One) or Mac ind Óg, is a prominent figure in Irish mythology, associated with love, youth, beauty, and poetry. He is a member of the Tuatha Dé Danann, the divine race who preceded the Gaels in Ireland. Oenghus's parentage is somewhat complex, with varying accounts suggesting either the Dagda or Elatha as his father, and Boann as his mother. He is renowned for his charming demeanor, handsome appearance, and his ability to influence matters of the heart. Oenghus's palace, Brú na Bóinne (Newgrange), is a place of immense beauty and magical significance, where he spends his time surrounded by birds that sing sweet melodies. He possesses the power to manipulate emotions and inspire love in others, and he often intervenes in the affairs of mortals, particularly those seeking romantic fulfillment. He is also associated with dreams and visions, and can communicate through these means. Oenghus's role extends beyond mere romantic love; he embodies the ideals of youthful vitality, artistic inspiration, and the power of beauty to transform the world. His stories often involve quests, trickery, and the fulfillment of destinies, highlighting his active participation in the unfolding of mythical events. He represents a potent force for good, promoting harmony and affection wherever he goes. His influence is felt in tales of forbidden love, arranged marriages, and the pursuit of true happiness. Oenghus's association with Brú na Bóinne also links him to themes of fertility, abundance, and the cyclical nature of life.
subtitle: God of Love, Youth, and Beauty

keyMyths.title: The Wooing of Étaín
keyMyths.description: Oenghus aids Midir in winning back his wife, Étaín, from King Eochaid Airem. He uses his magical abilities and cunning to help Midir overcome the king's challenges.

keyMyths.title: The Changing of Ailill and Findabair
keyMyths.description: Oenghus transforms Ailill and Findabair into swans so they can be together, escaping a forced marriage. This highlights his power over love and destiny.

keyMyths.title: Oenghus's Birth
keyMyths.description: Boann conceives Oenghus with either the Dagda or Elatha. The circumstances of his birth are often shrouded in secrecy and involve magical interventions to conceal the affair.

cultural.modernLegacy: Oenghus, or Angus, continues to inspire artists, writers, and those interested in Celtic spirituality. He is often invoked in matters of love, beauty, and creativity, representing the eternal youth and romantic ideals found in Irish mythology. His stories are retold and reinterpreted in contemporary literature and art, ensuring his continued presence in popular culture.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Óengus's birth is shrouded in intrigue and divine intervention. He is typically described as the son of the Dagda, the chief of the Tuatha Dé Danann, and Boann, the goddess associated with the River Boyne. However, some accounts suggest Elatha of the Fomorians as his father, adding to the complexity of his origins. The circumstances of his conception involve the Dagda using his magic to halt the sun's movement, creating a night long enough to consummate his relationship with Boann, who was married to Elcmar at the time. This extended night allowed Boann to conceal her pregnancy from her husband for an unusually long period. Óengus's birth is thus steeped in magic, deception, and the power of the divine, setting the stage for his future role as a significant figure in Irish mythology.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Óengus features prominently in several key Irish myths. One of the most famous is the tale of Caer Ibormeith, a beautiful maiden who appeared to him in a dream. He fell deeply in love and searched for her relentlessly. After a year of searching, he discovered that Caer was transformed into a swan every other year. Óengus eventually found her at a lake and, by transforming himself into a swan, joined her. Together, they flew away, singing a song that put all who heard it to sleep. Another significant myth involves Óengus's acquisition of Brú na Bóinne (Newgrange) from his father, the Dagda, through trickery and clever negotiation. These stories highlight Óengus's association with love, beauty, and his cunning intelligence.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Óengus's legacy extends beyond his direct appearances in mythological tales. He represents the ideals of youth, beauty, and romantic love, making him a popular figure in Irish culture. His association with Brú na Bóinne, a significant archaeological site, further connects him to the ancient history and landscape of Ireland. In modern times, Óengus inspires artists, writers, and those interested in Celtic spirituality. He is often invoked in matters of love, beauty, and creativity, representing the eternal youth and romantic ideals found in Irish mythology. His stories are retold and reinterpreted in contemporary literature and art, ensuring his continued presence in popular culture.

shortDescription: Irish god of love, youth, and beauty, Óengus is a member of the Tuatha Dé Danann. Known for his charm and involvement in romantic tales.
